candidate_problems.text: How can 'structural redundancy of enforcement' be operationalized and measured independently of popularity, legitimacy, and coercive intensity? The conjecture hinges on redundancy as the key causal variable, but it remains unclear what counts as a distinct enforcement channel, how overlap versus true redundancy should be modeled, and what observable indicators would distinguish distributed self-reproduction from merely widespread but centrally induced compliance.

What are the threshold dynamics and failure modes by which a distributed norm transitions from resilient to rapidly collapsible? The conjecture suggests many small feedback loops create durability, yet open questions remain about whether collapse requires simultaneous weakening across subsystems, whether some loops are disproportionately load-bearing, and how expectation cascades, network topology, and substitution among enforcement mechanisms determine tipping points.

When does multifunctionality stabilize a norm versus make it vulnerable to broad systemic change? Tying a norm to many system functions may create redundancy, but it may also couple the norm to shocks that propagate across family, status, law, language, and markets at once. An unresolved tension is whether deep embedding generally increases resilience or whether, in some extreme cases, high interdependence creates correlated failure and faster collapse than a narrower but more modular norm would face.
